title: Time quasi-periodic gravity water waves in finite depth
speaker: Massimiliano Berti
abstract: We prove the existence and the linear stability of Cantor families of small amplitude time quasi-periodic standing water waves solutions, namely periodic and even in the space variable $x$, of a bi-dimensional ocean with finite depth under the action of pure gravity. Such a result holds for all the values of the depth parameter in a set of asymptotically full measure. This is a small divisor problem.

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian finite-depth water-wave formulation
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates irrotational incompressible gravity water waves over a flat bottom, introduces the surface variables (η,ψ), the Zakharov Hamiltonian, and the Dirichlet-Neumann operator governing the nonlinear evolution.

proof_stages.label: Reversible standing-wave phase space
proof_stages.summary: Reversibility, evenness in the horizontal variable, Neumann-wall interpretation, and zero-mean conventions reduce the problem to reversible standing waves with no zero Fourier mode.

proof_stages.label: Linear modes and candidate invariant tori
proof_stages.summary: Linearization at the rest state diagonalizes into oscillators with dispersion ω_j(h)=sqrt(j tanh(hj)), and finite superpositions over chosen tangential modes give the quasi-periodic tori to be continued nonlinearly.

proof_stages.label: Small-divisor and quasilinear obstructions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker isolates the main difficulties: derivative-losing quasilinear nonlinearities, sublinear clustered gravity frequencies, weak exponential dependence on the depth parameter, nonintegrability, and the absence of general long-time or global results in the periodic pure-gravity case.

proof_stages.label: Cantor-family existence theorem
proof_stages.summary: For any finite tangential set and small amplitudes, the theorem gives small quasi-periodic reversible standing solutions close to the linear tori for most depth parameters h, equivalently on large-measure sections in amplitude-depth space.

proof_stages.label: Reducibility and Floquet asymptotics
proof_stages.summary: A key PDE/KAM ingredient is that the linearization along the constructed torus is conjugated by bounded quasi-periodic maps to constant diagonal oscillators with purely imaginary Floquet exponents μ_j whose sharp asymptotic expansion is computed.

proof_stages.label: Nash-Moser inversion scheme
proof_stages.summary: Because the naive implicit-function theorem fails through small divisors and derivative loss, the construction is organized as a fast Newton/Nash-Moser/KAM iteration requiring inverse estimates for the linearized operator at approximate quasi-periodic solutions.

proof_stages.label: Depth transversality and measure estimates
proof_stages.summary: Analytic dependence on h and a torsion/nondegeneracy property of the finite tangential frequency curve imply that each relevant divisor has a nontrivial derivative of bounded order, yielding lower bounds outside a zero-measure resonant Cantor set.

examples.name: Infinite-depth gravity-wave dispersion
examples.note: The case h=∞ is used to make the symbol become |ξ|^{1/2} and later to contrast the no-parameter resonance problem with finite depth; it clarifies but does not drive the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes the finite-depth dispersion relation to h=∞, computing it as √|ξ| and identifying the resulting frequencies √j and their resonance issue
